questions.question: What does "framing" really mean in photography, as taught here?
options: Shooting your subject through a doorway, arch or gap in the branches
Controlling everything inside your four edges — what's in, what's out, and how it relates
Adding a printed border to the final image
Locking exposure before you recompose
questions.reviewHint: Framing is the craft of deciding what sits inside the frame and how it works together — not one trick.

questions.question: The single most useful framing habit for a beginner is to…
options: Always find an archway to shoot through
Place the subject dead centre every time
Run your eye round all four edges before shooting, clearing mergers and distractions
Use the widest lens you own
questions.reviewHint: Checking the edges for mergers, bright distractions and half-cut objects is the habit worth making automatic.

questions.question: Before anything else, a strong frame needs…
options: A frame-within-a-frame element
A clear subject — you can name what the photo is of
The highest possible megapixel count
A fast shutter speed
questions.reviewHint: Decide what the photograph is of first; everything else serves the subject.

questions.question: "Balance" in framing is best understood as…
options: Keeping the camera level on a tripod
Setting equal aperture and shutter values
Always centring the subject
The distribution of visual weight — a matter of feel you can deliberately break
questions.reviewHint: Balance is feel, not symmetry — once you can feel it, deliberate imbalance becomes a tool.

questions.question: What is the role of the "frame-within-a-frame" (a doorway or arch) in good framing?
options: The definition of framing itself
One minor optional technique — useful when it quietly does the real jobs, not the headline
A required element in every well-framed photo
The first thing to look for in any scene
questions.reviewHint: Use it when it genuinely helps; don't let hunting for an arch override a strong subject.

questions.question: Your subject should usually be…
options: The darkest object in the scene
Always smaller than the frame element
The strongest thing in the frame, unless you decide otherwise
Placed outside the frame
questions.reviewHint: Watch that nothing brighter, sharper or louder steals the eye from your subject.

questions.question: "Pathways" in a frame (lines, light, repeated shapes) are used to…
options: Add a printed border
Carry the eye toward the subject — leading lines are one kind, not a rule
Increase dynamic range
Freeze motion
questions.reviewHint: Pathways guide attention; sometimes you want the viewer to wander instead.

questions.question: A supporting element (foreground, background, props) earns its place if it…
options: Is brighter than the subject
Fills every empty corner
Is always a natural frame
Adds context, depth, a pathway or balance — otherwise remove it
questions.reviewHint: Keep asking what each element is doing; lose it if the answer is "nothing".

questions.question: Tonal or contrast "breaks" in a frame should be…
options: Always removed completely
Noticed and judged — do they help or pull the eye off the subject?
Always placed in the centre
Ignored entirely
questions.reviewHint: A strong break is sometimes the whole point; notice it and decide on purpose.

questions.question: Why learn these framing habits at all?
options: So you can apply them as fixed rules forever
So every photo has an archway in it
So you never have to check the edges again
So they become instinct — and you can break them deliberately, knowing why
questions.reviewHint: Rules you've absorbed are the ones you get to break well — that's framing as a craft.
